summary: Should the MCP server tarball SHA be signed inside the DSSE attestation predicate at release time, or revert to in-tree bundle with tighter freshness gate?

options.description: Sign npm tarball sha512 inside DSSE predicate at release time

summary: Ship npm-shrinkwrap.json alongside plugin.json to enforce integrity hashes on first-install of mcp-server + transitive deps?

options.description: Ship npm-shrinkwrap.json regenerated on each plugin version bump

rationale: Operator decision 2026-05-26 via decision-rubric walkthrough. Picked over (B) npm-native provenance to keep ONE trust anchor (operator's trusted-reviewers.yaml pubkey set, already used for v6 review attestations) rather than introducing GitHub OIDC as a second anchor. Picked over (C) revert-to-in-tree because AISDLC-385 just eliminated that friction. Picked over (D) status-quo because npm registry compromise IS in the threat model. Reuses existing AI-SDLC v6 Merkle envelope infrastructure; composes with DEC-0002 shrinkwrap layer for defense-in-depth.

Operator decision 2026-05-26 via decision-rubric walkthrough. Picked over (B) npm-audit-signatures because registry signature coverage is uneven across transitive deps. Picked over (C) pin-direct-only because partial mitigation is worst-of-both. Picked over (D) status-quo because no install-time integrity guarantee for any dep is unacceptable for MCP server's blast radius (operator credentials + repo write access). Patch-velocity cost mitigated by existing Dependabot/Renovate auto-PR flow that drives transitive bumps. Composes with DEC-0001 signed-tarball layer.

summary: identityclass-classification-disagreement: did-compiler.ts ic() defaults to 'evolving'; canonical RFC-0028 §7.1 says 'core'

options.description: Align did-compiler.ts ic() default to canonical 'core'
Carve documented cross-layer exemption keeping 'evolving' as DID-scoring default
Revise canonical RFC-0028 §7.1 taxonomy to per-domain defaults
